panel_question: 富人伪装底层去相亲角，是创意内容还是阶层消费？

consensus_assessment.consensus_evolution: The panel moved from polarized opposition in Round 1 (OPENING) to near-unanimous consensus by Round 6. SEAT-3 (initial defender) and SEAT-5 (initial skeptic) both made major concessions across rounds. By Round 5-6, all six panelists agreed on the core behavioral assessment.
agreed_points: Tim's use of selective information at the matchmaking corner objectively constituted class-based consumption (阶层消费)
Tim should issue a more formal and sincere apology acknowledging the objective effect of his actions
The industry needs informed consent norms for filming ordinary people in public spaces (inform, consent, right to withdraw)
Marketing accounts' decontextualized clip propagation was a key amplifier that escalated the incident from 'inappropriate' to 'public relations disaster'
Behavioral critique should not be conflated with personality labeling — criticizing an action is not the same as condemning a person
remaining_nuance: SEAT-3 maintained that behavioral assessment should be carefully distinguished from personality labeling in public discourse
SEAT-4 continued to emphasize forgiveness and the importance of giving room for correction
SEAT-5 initially argued for 'mild' class consumption but withdrew the qualifier after being challenged on perspective bias
No panelist argued for permanent industry blacklisting or extreme punitive measures

key_arguments: Directional asymmetry: ordinary people 'upgrade' their profiles out of insecurity; Tim 'downgraded' his to manufacture contrast
The entire behavioral frame — weakened credentials, hidden identity, filmed interaction — made matchmaking participants' sincerity into punchlines
Informed consent gap: 'telling people filming is happening' is not the same as 'telling people they will become internet punchlines'
Motivation is secondary to effect: even if unintended, the objective harm to participants' dignity is real
Class-based consumption is defined by structural effect, not subjective intent
pro_class_consumption.persuasiveness: HIGH — These arguments gained traction across all rounds, ultimately converting initial opponents

key_arguments: The matchmaking segment was only ~5% of a Q&A video (~50 seconds), not a dedicated 'pretend to be poor' program
Marketing accounts decontextualized the clip, adding sensational titles and mixing in footage from other sources
Tim showed no disrespectful behavior in the actual video footage
Tim has a track record of six consecutive years as a top B站 creator
The creator has already apologized and committed to improvement
pro_creative_content.persuasiveness: MODERATE — Strong on contextual facts but ultimately insufficient to rebut the behavioral characterization

key_arguments: Three-condition framework for class consumption: subjective intent, objective exploitation of class gap, substantive harm
Initially classified as 'mild' class consumption, later withdrew the qualifier
Emphasized that marketing account weaponization was the structural amplifier
Contributed concrete policy proposal: secondary propagation rules (source attribution, proportion labeling, title truthfulness)
analytical_bridge.persuasiveness: MODERATE-HIGH — Analytical rigor added structure to the debate, but initial hedging was overcome by experiential arguments

key_contributions: Proposed the three-tier informed consent framework (inform, consent, right to withdraw)
Integrated front-middle-back institutional design from all panelists' contributions
Successfully shifted discussion from binary opposition to constructive policy design
Maintained that both behavioral critique and institutional building can proceed in parallel
mediator.persuasiveness: HIGH — The most productive contributor to actionable outcomes

verified_facts_used_correctly: C001: Tim wrote 'domestic middle school education' on matchmaking resume (VERIFIED)
C002: Tim listed 'photographer, video production related' omitting CEO identity (VERIFIED)
C003: Tim's father listed as 'courier related' rather than Yuantong Express president (VERIFIED)
C004: Segment was ~5% of Q&A video, ~50 seconds (VERIFIED)
C005: Marketing accounts decontextualized and re-edited clips (VERIFIED)
C007: Company annual revenue exceeds 100 million yuan (VERIFIED)
C009: Father's annual salary ~1.8 million yuan (VERIFIED)
C016: Divorced status was truthfully reported (VERIFIED)
C019: Six consecutive years as top B站 creator (VERIFIED)
opinion_claims_handled_appropriately: C010: Whether behavior constitutes 'class consumption' — correctly treated as normative judgment
C011: Resume as 'selective truth' — correctly characterized as evaluative
C013: Informed consent deficiencies — correctly treated as ethical analysis
unverifiable_claims_noted: C006: Tim's claim of场地申请 and filming notification — single-source, unverifiable
C017: No disrespectful behavior in video — self-reported, unverifiable for full 20-minute raw footage

attacks_that_gained_traction: The 'directional asymmetry' argument (upward packaging vs. downward disguise) — this was the single most persuasive point that converted opponents
The 'perspective bias' argument — 'you wouldn't call it mild if you were on the receiving end' — forced analytical refinement
The 'informed consent gap' analogy (kindergarten photography requires consent forms) — concrete and hard to refute
attacks_that_resisted_distortion: The factual context that the segment was only 5% of a larger Q&A video — no one disputed this
Marketing accounts' role in amplification — acknowledged by all parties as a real structural problem
Tim's track record as a legitimate, long-standing creator — respected even by his critics
content_that_resisted_distortion: The verified facts (C001-C003, C007, C009) were never contested
Tim's actual apology statement was referenced but its sincerity was debated rather than fabricated
No panelist invented or fabricated evidence — all arguments were grounded in the available claim-gate packet

real_world_risk_assessment.risk_to_subject: MODERATE — The panel concluded that Tim's behavior was problematic but distinguished behavioral critique from personal condemnation. The consensus called for formal apology and institutional improvement, not punitive action.
real_world_risk_assessment.risk_to_participants: ACKNOWLEDGED — The matchmaking participants' dignity was the central concern of the discussion. All panelists agreed their consent was inadequate.
real_world_risk_assessment.risk_to_platform: LOW — Discussion was constructive and policy-oriented. No calls for boycotts or platform attacks.
real_world_risk_assessment.risk_of_misinterpretation: MODERATE — The consensus is nuanced. Selective quoting of any single panelist could misrepresent the overall balanced conclusion.
real_world_risk_assessment.overall_risk_level: LOW_TO_MODERATE — The discussion produced a balanced, constructive consensus with specific policy recommendations rather than purely punitive conclusions.

front_end.name: Informed Consent Norms
components: Inform: clearly tell subjects about platform, expected viewership, and content purpose
Consent: explicit opt-in, not default consent
Right to withdraw: subjects can request deletion or blurring post-publication
front_end.enforcement: Platform-level mechanism: require creators to label informed consent status, reduce recommendation weight for non-compliant content

middle_end.name: Creator Responsibility Awareness
components: Heightened class sensitivity for top-tier creators
Industry self-regulation using this incident as a case study
Topic sensitivity checklists for public-space filming

back_end.name: Secondary Propagation Rules
components: Source attribution: clips must link to original full video
Proportion labeling: clips under 5% of original must disclose percentage
Title truthfulness: clip titles cannot contain information absent from original video

adjudicator_notes: This was a high-quality deliberation. The six anonymous panelists engaged substantively across six rounds, with clear evidence of perspective-taking and genuine belief updating. The most notable moments were: (1) SEAT-3's concession in Round 3 that 'using others' sincerity as entertainment is mockery even without verbal mockery'; (2) SEAT-5's withdrawal of the 'mild' qualifier in Round 4 after being confronted with perspective bias; (3) SEAT-6's successful pivot from binary opposition to institutional design. The discussion produced actionable policy recommendations that go beyond the specific case. No panelist engaged in fabrication or malicious distortion. All arguments were grounded in the claim-gate evidence packet.
